publication.title: BindPredict: a random forest model for predicting protein–ligand binding residues from sequence features.
publication.authors: Example A, Demonstration B, Fictitious C.

dataset.availability: Yes. Training and test datasets are available in the GitHub repository at https://github.com/example-org/bindpredict/data/.
dataset.provenance: Protein–ligand binding residue annotations were extracted from the BioLiP database (version 2023-01). The final dataset contains 3,842 positive (binding) residue instances and 38,120 negative (non-binding) instances across 512 non-redundant proteins.
dataset.redundancy: Sequence redundancy was reduced using cd-hit at a 30% pairwise identity threshold. Proteins with >30% identity to any entry in the test set were removed from training to prevent data leakage.
dataset.splits: Data were split into training (70%), validation (10%), and independent test (20%) sets by stratified random sampling, maintaining a consistent positive-to-negative ratio of 1:10.

optimization.algorithm: Random Forest classifier (scikit-learn 1.2.2). An ensemble of 500 decision trees was trained using bagging with bootstrap sampling and random feature subsets at each split.
optimization.features: Each residue was represented by a 66-dimensional feature vector: 20-dimensional amino acid composition in a sliding window of ±5 residues, PSSM scores from PSI-BLAST (3 iterations against UniRef90), and 6 physicochemical descriptors.
optimization.encoding: The sequence window of 11 residues was encoded using PSSM values (20 aa × 11 positions = 220 values) concatenated with mean physicochemical property values for the window.
optimization.config: Yes. Key hyperparameters: n_estimators=500, max_features='sqrt', max_depth=None, min_samples_split=2, class_weight='balanced'. Selected by 5-fold cross-validation grid search on the training set.
optimization.parameters: The Random Forest model has approximately 2.1 million decision nodes across all 500 trees. Feature importance scores are reported in Supplementary Table S2.
optimization.fitting: Training AUROC was 0.97; independent test AUROC was 0.83. The gap is consistent with Random Forest behaviour on imbalanced data and is discussed in Section 3.4.
optimization.regularization: No explicit regularization was applied. Overfitting was mitigated through bootstrap aggregation (bagging) and limiting feature subsets at each node split (max_features='sqrt').
optimization.meta: No. A single Random Forest model was used without stacking or boosting.

model.availability: Yes. Trained model weights (pickle format), inference code, and a Docker container are available at https://github.com/example-org/bindpredict/.
model.interpretability: Partially transparent. Feature importance scores derived from mean decrease in Gini impurity are reported for all 66 features (Supplementary Table S2).
model.output: Binary classification per residue: binding (1) or non-binding (0), with an associated probability score in [0, 1].
model.duration: Training time: approximately 12 minutes on a single Intel Xeon E5-2680 CPU core (no GPU required). Inference on a 300-residue protein takes <1 second.

evaluation.availability: Yes. All evaluation scripts and result files are provided in the GitHub repository at https://github.com/example-org/bindpredict/evaluation/.
evaluation.measure: AUROC, MCC, precision, recall, and F1-score. MCC was chosen as the primary metric because it is robust to class imbalance (positive:negative ratio 1:10).
evaluation.method: 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set for model selection. Final performance was assessed on a fully held-out independent test set (20% of total data).
evaluation.comparison: BindPredict was compared to three prior methods: BindN+, SitePredict, and a naive majority-class baseline. BindPredict achieved a test MCC of 0.48, outperforming both.
evaluation.confidence: Mean and standard deviation of AUROC and MCC across 5 cross-validation folds are reported (Table 2). A paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirmed statistically significant improvement over BindN+ (p=0.018) and SitePredict (p=0.031).
